symbols: six wings — the seraphim of Isaiah's vision, two covering the face, two covering the feet, two for flying
golden halo — the radiance of divine presence
large luminous eyes — the seraphim's perpetual gaze upon the glory of God
blue and gold feathers — the colors of heaven and divine glory
description: A church fresco or mural detail showing a frontal view of a seraph — a six-winged angelic being. The figure has a youthful androgynous face with large, dark, luminous eyes and curly dark hair, surrounded by a golden halo. Six wings extend from the figure — two above the head, two at the sides, and two below — painted in rich blues, greens, and golds. The background is warm gold and reddish-brown. The style is characteristic of late 19th-century Russian or Byzantine-revival church decoration.
theological_description: The seraphim appear in Isaiah's throne-room vision: 'Above him stood the seraphim. Each had six wings: with two he covered his face, and with two he covered his feet, and with two he flew. And one called to another and said: Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory!' (ISA.6.2-3). The seraphim (literally 'burning ones') are the highest order of angelic beings, standing in the immediate presence of God. Their covering of face and feet signifies that even the most exalted creatures must veil themselves before the overwhelming holiness of God. Their threefold 'Holy' (the Trisagion) became the foundation of Christian liturgical worship, echoed in Revelation: 'Day and night they never cease to say, Holy, holy, holy, is the Lord God Almighty, who was and is and is to come!' (REV.4.8). The seraph's direct gaze in this icon invites the viewer into the same worshipful awe that overwhelmed Isaiah.
